title: The ₹500 Question
subtitle: Foundations: likelihood × consequence

hook.metaphor: You already understand credit risk. Every time a friend asks to borrow money, your brain runs a tiny risk model: *will I actually get this back?* That instinct — scaled up with data, software, and rules — is the entire field.
hook.scenario: Your cousin texts: “Can I borrow ₹50,000? I’ll pay you back next month — promise.” He’s missed paying you back twice before. Do you lend it?

learn: **Credit risk is the chance that someone you lent money to doesn’t pay it back** — on time, or at all. When they fail to pay as agreed, that’s called a **default**. A bank lending ₹500 crore and you lending a cousin ₹500 are doing the same thing, just at different scale.

Every lending decision has two parts. **Likelihood** — how *likely* is it to go wrong? (Your cousin who ghosted you twice = high likelihood.) And **consequence** (or impact) — how *much* does it hurt if it does? (₹500 you’ll shrug off; ₹50,000 you won’t.) **Risk = likelihood × consequence.** A tiny chance of a catastrophe and a near-certain tiny loss can carry the *same* risk.

This is why lenders don’t just ask “will they pay?” — they ask “how likely, and how bad if not?” A reliable friend borrowing your house deposit can be *riskier* than a flaky friend borrowing lunch money, because the consequence is so much larger.

Credit risk is one branch of a bigger family of business risks. **Operational risk** is things going wrong inside the machine (a system fails, someone makes a mistake). **Market risk** is losses from prices moving (interest rates, exchange rates). **Fraud risk** is someone deceiving you on purpose. Knowing which box a problem falls into is the first job of anyone working in risk — because each is managed differently.

Worldwide, lenders are held to this discipline by regulators: India’s **RBI**, Australia’s **APRA**, and New Zealand’s **RBNZ** all require lenders to measure and hold money against the credit risk they take on. The vocabulary is global; only the rulebook changes by country.
bridge: So at work, you can reframe almost any “will we get burned?” decision as **likelihood × consequence** — and push back when someone fixates on one and ignores the other. “It’s unlikely” isn’t reassuring if the consequence is ruinous; “it’s a small amount” isn’t safe if it happens constantly.

interactions.title: Would you lend?
interactions.prompt: Your cousin has missed repaying you twice before, and ₹50,000 is real money to you. Using likelihood × consequence, what’s the soundest call?

options.label: Lend the full ₹50,000 — he promised.
options.feedback: A promise doesn’t change the *likelihood*. His track record (missed twice) says high chance of default, and ₹50,000 is a high consequence for you. High × high = the riskiest possible call.

options.label: Lend a smaller amount you can afford to lose.
options.feedback: Exactly the lever a real lender pulls: you can’t easily change the likelihood, so you reduce the *consequence*. Smaller loan = smaller impact if it defaults. This is “risk appetite” in action.

options.label: Lend it, but ask for his bike as security.
options.feedback: Not bad — collateral cuts your *consequence* (you recover something on default). Real lenders do exactly this. Just make sure the security is actually worth the loan and you can claim it.

options.label: Refuse — never lend to anyone, ever.
options.feedback: Avoiding all risk also avoids all reward (and all friendships intact). Risk management isn’t “say no to everything” — it’s pricing and sizing the risk you take.

interactions.title: Which kind of risk is it?
interactions.prompt: Drag each situation into the risk family it belongs to. (This sorting instinct is the daily job of a risk professional.)

items.label: A borrower stops repaying their car loan.
A power outage takes the loan-approval system offline.
Interest rates jump and the loan book loses value.
Someone applies using a stolen identity.
A small business can’t make its repayments after a bad season.
A staff member fat-fingers an extra zero on a payout.

interactions.title: The Approval Dial
interactions.prompt: You’re the lender now. Drag the sliders and watch the risk score, the heat-map cell, and the verdict change. Notice how the same loan flips from Approve to Decline as likelihood (missed payments) and consequence (loan size) move.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Credit risk is just ‘will I get my money back?’ — and how worried I should be is the chance it goes wrong times how much it would hurt.”

checkpoint.q: Risk is best thought of as…
options: How likely something is to go wrong
How badly it would hurt if it did
checkpoint.explain: Risk combines both: a likely-but-tiny loss and an unlikely-but-huge loss can carry the same risk.

checkpoint.q: A borrower failing to repay as agreed is called a…
checkpoint.explain: Default = failing to meet the agreed repayment obligation.

checkpoint.q: Someone applies for a loan using a stolen identity. That’s primarily…
checkpoint.explain: Deception on purpose = fraud risk. It often rides alongside credit risk, but it’s managed differently.
